correct: A stunning, scientifically accurate macro photograph of an Iris versicolor (Blue Flag Iris) in natural morning light. The flower exhibits a classic violet-blue hue. The three outer sepals (falls) are elongated and gracefully arching, featuring a greenish-yellow patch at the base heavily veined with purple. The three inner petals (standards) are erect and significantly shorter than the sepals but clearly visible and violet-colored. The texture of the petals is velvety. The background is a soft, creamy green bokeh of wetland foliage. No text or labels.
deceptive_a: A close-up botanical illustration style image of an Iris virginica (Southern Blue Flag), lit by soft diffuse sunlight. The flower features deep violet-blue petals and sepals. The sepals are spreading and slightly drooping with a bright yellow, pubescent signal patch. The standard petals are upright and narrow. The flower appears robust and slightly larger than a typical wild iris, with intricate purple veining running through the yellow throat of the falls. Rain droplets rest on the petal surface. Shallow depth of field with a blurred dark green background. No text or overlays.
deceptive_b: A high-resolution nature photograph presenting an Iris setosa (Arctic Iris), though unusually large in proportion. The flower has deep purple-blue sepals that are wide at the base and taper outward. Contrary to typical setosa traits, the inner upright petals are prominent and elongated rather than reduced, matching the specific physical dimensions of a larger iris variety. The lighting highlights the iridescence of the petal texture against a sun-dappled marsh background. The image is pure photography with no diagrams or text.
